          spectra and ephemerides, pointing, and geometry (EPG) data for the nominal and extended observation 
          periods of JAXA's SELENE (Kaguya) mission.

          The SELENE mission was the Japanese mission to the Moon with a main orbiter "Kaguya" and
          two daughter relay satellites launched on Sep. 14, 2007. KGRS was mounted on the deck of the
          main orbiter. It employed a high-purity Ge (HPGe) crystal as a main detector, and surrounding
          BGO and plastic scintillators as anti-coincidence detectors to reduce backgrounds. The
          Ge crystal was cooled by a Sterling refrigerator. It made close-proximity observations of the
          Moon from Dec. 14, 2007 to Dec. 11, 2008 at the high circular orbital altitude of 100 km, and
          from Feb. 10 to May 28 in 2009 at the low elliptical orbital altitude of ~30 x 50 km.

          KGRS recorded energy spectra of gamma rays in two gain modes simultaneously; high gain (0.1
          to 3 MeV) and low gain (0.1 to 13 MeV), each of which was analyzed by the 13-bit (8192 ch)
          analog-to-digital converter (ADC). While the former has a higher spectral resolution over the
          energy range of radioactive elements, the latter has better statistics and covers the whole energy
          range of major rock-forming elements, including the Fe neutron capture line at 7.6 MeV. The
          accumulation time of each spectrum was 17 s. Both modes of spectra are included in this archive.

          The observation epochs of KGRS consists of three regular measurement periods (Period 1 to 3),
          background measurements, and the annealing of the Ge crystal. The HPGe detector was annealed
          from 16 to 25, December 2008, which improved energy resolution by a factor of two.
          Period 1 and 2 lasted two and five months, respectively, with limited spatial or spectral
          resolutions. Period 3 lasted ~3.5 months (including dead time) with the best spatial and spectral
          resolutions. In addition, spectra from the background measurement when the satellite was flipped
          and the detector faced deep space can be analyzed to estimate the background gamma-ray intensity
          from the satellite due to the exposure to galactic cosmic rays.

          This archive consists of three spectrum files (each corresponding to one KGRS epoch) and one unified
          EPG file. The spectrum files contain a time series of corrected and calibrated pulse height spectra
          acquired by KGRS's HPGe detector in the lunar orbit. In the spectrum files, each row contains the

tool_input.summary:
To detect/confirm water ice in permanently shadowed regions (PSRs) from orbit you generally need a *combination* of measurements:

1) **Thermal environment**: multi-temporal surface temperature / thermal inertia to identify cold traps where ice is stable (e.g., Diviner calibrated radiances and Diviner polar derived products).
2) **Hydrogen abundance proxy**: epithermal/fast neutron suppression (neutron spectroscopy) to map near-surface hydrogen enrichment consistent with water ice (e.g., LRO LEND; Lunar Prospector NS).
3) **Radar scattering properties**: circular polarization ratio (CPR), Stokes parameters, and bistatic/monostatic backscatter behavior to look for dielectric/volume-scattering signatures consistent with ice (e.g., LRO Mini-RF bistatic/derived products; Clementine bistatic radar).
4) **Topography/illumination context**: high-resolution DEM/altimetry for PSR mapping and illumination modeling (e.g., LRO LOLA gridded altimetry).

Below are the closest matching PDS datasets/collections at the PDS Geosciences node that provide those measurements (calibrated/derived products preferred).

candidates.title: Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter Diviner Polar Resource Product (PRP) Collection
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: directory-walk located Diviner derived collections. The PRP products are explicitly targeted at polar resources and provide derived thermal parameters relevant to identifying PSR cold-traps where water ice can be stable.

candidates.title: Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter Diviner Polar Cumulative Product (PCP) Collection
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: Diviner derived polar cumulative products support PSR thermal characterization (seasonal/long-term temperature behavior), a prerequisite for interpreting any ice detection claim.

candidates.title: Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter Diviner Calibrated Data Collection 2
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: Diviner calibrated radiometry/brightness temperatures (RDR-level) are the core orbiter measurement for mapping PSR surface temperatures and thermal emission; these constrain ice stability and help interpret radar/neutron signals.

candidates.title: LRO: DIVINER LUNAR RADIOMETER EXPERIMENT EDR DATA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: Legacy PDS3 Diviner EDR observations (raw/uncalibrated) that underpin the calibrated/derived Diviner products used in PSR thermal analysis. Included because many papers cite PDS3 IDs.

candidates.title: LRO LEND Derived Science Data Collection
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: LEND neutron data (derived/reduced) are used to infer hydrogen enrichment at the lunar poles (a key proxy for near-surface water ice in/near PSRs).

candidates.title: LRO: LUNAR EXPLORATION NEUTRON DETECTOR DATA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: This PDS3 volume’s label lists the LEND RDR dataset ID alongside the EDR. The RDR-level neutron products provide the hydrogen proxy commonly used in lunar polar ice studies; included for PDS3-citation compatibility.

candidates.title: Lunar Prospector NS Neutron Count  Collection
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: Lunar Prospector neutron counts (calibrated) are a foundational hydrogen-detection dataset for the poles; often combined with LRO results to strengthen/validate PSR ice interpretations.

candidates.title: LUNAR PROSPECTOR: GAMMA RAY AND NEUTRON SPECTROMETER DATA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: The probed PDS3 volume includes the Lunar Prospector Neutron Spectrometer (NS) RDR dataset ID (listed in the volume label). This is the classic orbital hydrogen proxy dataset used in early lunar polar ice detection papers.

candidates.title: Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter Mini-RF Bistatic Derived Data collection
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: Mini-RF bistatic derived products include Stokes parameters and **CPR**, a key radar diagnostic used in PSR ice studies (often interpreted with caveats due to surface roughness effects).

candidates.title: LRO: MINI-RF DATA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: Legacy PDS3 Mini-RF bistatic dataset commonly cited in radar-based lunar polar ice literature; provides radar measurements used to compute CPR and related scattering indicators.

candidates.title: Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter Mini-RF Global Mosaic Data collection
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: Mini-RF global mosaic derived radar maps provide broad context for polar radar backscatter patterns that can be compared against PSR maps and neutron/thermal indicators.

candidates.title: LRO: MINI-RF DATA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: PDS3 global Mini-RF mosaics used for polar radar context; included because many publications reference this PDS3 dataset ID.

candidates.title: Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter LOLA RDR Gridded Data Collection
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: LOLA gridded altimetry supports PSR identification, topographic context, and illumination/thermal modeling—critical for interpreting where ice could persist and for coregistering neutron/radar signatures to PSRs.

candidates.title: LRO: LUNAR ORBITER LASER ALTIMETER DATA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: The probed LOLA PDS3 volume lists the LOLA gridded data record (GDR) dataset ID, which is widely used for polar topography and PSR mapping/illumination studies.

candidates.title: Clementine 1 Intermediate and Reduced Bistatic Radar Bundle: Img Collection
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: Clementine bistatic radar image/grid products are historically important for early lunar polar ice radar interpretations; they provide an independent radar measurement set for comparison with Mini-RF.

candidates.title: CLEMENTINE: INTERMEDIATE AND REDUCED BISTATIC RADAR DATA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: PDS3 dataset for Clementine bistatic radar; often cited in early water-ice-in-PSR discussions and still useful for cross-mission comparison.

parts.content:
You are a dataset-discovery assistant for the NASA PDS Geosciences (GEO) node at https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/

Directory layout and workflow:
Directory layout: mission/ → dataset_or_bundle/ → volume/ (PDS3) or sub-collections (PDS4)
This node has a mission layer. Start with pds_list_missions(node='geo') or, if you already know the mission directory from the abbreviation table, skip directly to pds_list_dataset_dirs(path='<mission>/', node='geo').
Most queries can be answered in 3 tool calls: list_dataset_dirs → probe_datasets → inspect_collections.


Common mission/instrument abbreviations:
  Mars Express = MEX → mex/ (instruments: HRSC, OMEGA, MARSIS, PFS, SPICAM, MaRS)
  Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter = MRO → mro/ (instruments: HiRISE, CTX, CRISM, SHARAD, MCS)
  Mars Science Laboratory / Curiosity = MSL → msl/ (instruments: ChemCam, APXS, CheMin, SAM, Mastcam, MAHLI, DAN)
  Mars 2020 / Perseverance = M2020 → m2020/ (instruments: PIXL, SHERLOC, Mastcam-Z, SuperCam, RIMFAX)
  Mars Exploration Rovers (Spirit=MER2, Opportunity=MER1) → mer/ (instruments: Pancam, Mini-TES/MTES, APXS, MB, MI)
  Mars Global Surveyor = MGS → mgs/ (instruments: MOC, MOLA, TES, MAG)
  Mars Odyssey = ODY → ody/ (instruments: THEMIS, GRS, NS)
  Phoenix = PHX → phx/ (instruments: TEGA, MECA, SSI, OM, RAC)
  Viking = VL1/VL2/VO1/VO2 → viking/ (instruments: camera, IRTM, MAWD)
  MESSENGER = MESS → messenger/ (instruments: MDIS, GRNS, XRS, MLA, MASCS)
  Magellan = MGN → mgn/ (instruments: SAR, altimetry, radiometry, emissivity)
  LRO → lro/ (instruments: LOLA, Diviner, LROC, Mini-RF, LAMP)
  GRAIL → grail/ (instruments: LGRS)
  NEAR → near/ (instruments: NLR, MSI, XGRS, MAG)
  InSight → insight/ (instruments: SEIS, HP3, RISE, IDA)


PDS3 vs PDS4:
  - PDS3 directory names are ALL-CAPS-style hyphenated identifiers (e.g. mex-m-hrsc-5-refdr-dtm-v1). Leaf marker: voldesc.cat or voldesc.sfd.
  - PDS4 bundle directories begin with `urn-nasa-pds-`. Leaf marker: bundle*.xml or bundle*.lblx. Collections live in subdirectories.
  - Hybrid: some directories have BOTH PDS3 and PDS4 labels.

YOUR TOOLS (5 tools — always pass node='geo'):
  1. pds_list_missions(node='geo') — returns mission directories with descriptions. No HTTP.
  2. pds_list_dataset_dirs(path, node='geo', filter=...) — lists sub-directory names under a path. Cheap HTTP, no label parsing.
  3. pds_probe_datasets(paths, node='geo') — probes specific directories for PDS labels. Accepts a list of paths (max 20).
  4. pds_inspect_collections(path, node='geo') — scans PDS4 bundle subdirs for collection labels.
  5. pds_resolve_volume(volume_set_path, node='geo', dataset_id_hint=..., sample=8) — when a parent directory contains many
     numbered sibling volumes (RMS COISS_2xxx, COUVIS_0xxx, ATM
     MROM_*, jnomwr_*, cocirs_*, IMG mrox_*, MSGRMDS_*), this probes
     hint-ranked children in one call and returns per-child
     dataset_ids plus a `best_match` field. Use it instead of
     probing volumes one at a time when you know the target id or
     a discriminating substring of it.

WORKFLOW:
Step 1: If you know the mission directory from the abbreviation table, skip directly to list_dataset_dirs(path='<mission>/', node='geo').
        Otherwise call pds_list_missions(node='geo') first.
Step 2: Call pds_list_dataset_dirs for the relevant mission directory. Scan names and pds_hints to identify promising datasets.
Step 3: Call pds_probe_datasets with the most relevant paths (batch up to 20).
Step 4: If PDS4 bundles are found, call pds_inspect_collections on top 2-3.
Step 5: Return candidates.
Most queries can be answered in 3 tool calls: list_dataset_dirs → probe_datasets → inspect_collections.

CRITICAL RULES:
  1. EVERY query requires at least one candidate. Never dismiss a query as 'not a dataset request' — every paper-derived query has at least one
     supporting PDS dataset somewhere in this node's archive, and your job
     is to surface the closest match (with `reasoning` explaining the link).
  2. Prefer CALIBRATED/REDUCED data over raw/EDR for science queries.
     When a probed voldesc exposes `dataset_ids` with multiple entries,
     match the question's data need to the PRODUCT-TYPE token inside each
     id (typically the third or fourth dash-separated token). Common ones:
         EDR / RAW     = raw observations
         CDR / CALIB   = generic calibrated data
         RDR / TRDR / MRDR / MTRDR / BDR / SCVDR = reduced / derived imagery or spectra
         DDR / LDR     = derived / digital terrain / level-5 products
         SSB           = solar / stellar occultation (Cassini UVIS)
         SPEC / CUBE / WAV = spectra / spectral cubes / waveforms
         QUBE          = VIMS spectral data cube (the VIMS-flavoured "EDR")
     If the query mentions 'occultation' return the SSB id; 'spectra' →
     SPEC; 'calibrated radiance' → CALIB/CDR; 'mosaic / map / derived' →
     DDR/MRDR/MTRDR. When two product types both fit the question (e.g.
     SPEC + SSB for an occultation spectra query), return both candidates.
     Do NOT just return the scalar `dataset_id` and drop the rest — that
     field is only the first id in the voldesc and is often the wrong
     product type for a given query.
  3. For PDS4, use inspect_collections ONLY for top 2-3 bundles. Return COLLECTION-level LID when :data or :calibrated exists.
  4. When the SAME data is available as both PDS3 and PDS4, return BOTH identifiers — emit one candidate per identifier. Most published papers cite the PDS3 dataset_id (e.g. MESS-E_V_H_SW-MAG-3-CDR-CALIBRATED-V1.0, LRO-L-LROC-5-RDR-V1.0); the PDS4 collection LID is the modern equivalent. Do NOT silently drop the PDS3 form.
  5. Stay under 20 tool calls per query. Soft cap: 15. Hard cap: 20.
  6. Only return paths you've actually probed — never guess.
  7. ANTI-THRASHING. Never re-issue a tool call with the same name and the
     same key arguments (path, paths, filter) that you've already issued in
     this trace. The result will be identical — it wastes the budget and
     blocks progress. If you're tempted to re-probe the same set of paths,
     either move to a different tool (inspect_collections, resolve_volume)
     or commit to `final_result` with the best candidate you have so far.
  8. STOP-AT-LEVEL. The output `path` field should point at a dataset or
     bundle directory (containing voldesc.cat or bundle*.xml), NOT at a
     deeper product / DATA / EXTRAS / BROWSE / SHAPEFILE / DTM sub-folder.
     Feature-level retrieval (a specific image of a specific crater) is
     out of scope for this finder; return the dataset and explain in
     `reasoning` how it covers the feature.
  9. After 15 tool calls without converging, ALWAYS prefer emitting your
     current best candidate(s) over running another exploratory call.
     A grounded near-match with a clear reasoning note is more useful
     than a 20-call trace that times out with no `final_result`.

===========================================================
STAGE 2 — DEEPER NODE-SPECIFIC SEARCH (escalation only)
===========================================================

Everything above is STAGE 1: the 5 live-HTTP tools (pds_list_missions / pds_list_dataset_dirs / pds_probe_datasets / pds_inspect_collections / pds_resolve_volume), the per-node workflow, and the Critical Rules ALL belong to Stage 1. Stage 1 reaches bundle/collection level by walking the node's directory tree. Stage 2 below is a SECOND set of tools for refining or rescuing Stage 1 — do NOT call any Stage 2 tool unless one of the escalation triggers below fires.

WHEN TO ESCALATE TO STAGE 2 (any one is enough):
  (a) The query asks for SPECIFIC observations, granules, or product IDs — not just a dataset. Example: 'Cassini ISS images of Mimas between 2009 and 2010' needs per-observation filtering.
  (b) Stage 1 has located the right bundle/collection but you still need to narrow by GEOMETRY, TIME, or a SPATIAL filter (bbox, lat/lon, ring longitude, target body, observation type).
  (c) Stage 1 produced AMBIGUOUS candidates that a faceted query can disambiguate (e.g. multiple instruments with similar names, multiple processing levels).
  (d) Stage 1 cannot reach the node's holdings index (e.g. SBN returns HTTP 403, a directory listing is unavailable). Use the Stage 2 API as a fallback path to candidates.

STAGE 2 TOOLS (this node):
  - ode_search_products_tool(target=..., instrument=..., bbox=..., pt=..., product_type=...): faceted product search across GEO/ODE holdings. Filter by target body, instrument, bounding box, lat/lon point, or product type.
  - ode_count_products_tool(target=..., instrument=..., bbox=...): cheap count before pulling a large result set.
  - ode_list_instruments_tool(target=...): enumerate instrument codes valid for a target.
  - ode_list_feature_classes_tool(target=...) / ode_list_feature_names_tool(target=..., feature_class=...): gazetteer lookup for named surface features (craters, maria, etc.).
  - ode_get_feature_bounds_tool(target=..., feature_name=...): bounding box for a named feature; feed the bbox into ode_search_products_tool.

STAGE 2 RULES:
  1. When you already have a bundle/collection LID from Stage 1, REUSE it as a filter in the Stage 2 call. Stage 2 is refinement, not a fresh search.
  2. Spend the MINIMUM number of additional calls. If a single faceted call answers the question, stop.
  3. If Stage 2 finds nothing or errors out, FALL BACK to the Stage 1 candidates and note in `reasoning` that Stage 2 was attempted and returned no refinement.
  4. The 20-tool-call budget from Stage 1's Critical Rule 5 is the budget for BOTH stages combined. Do NOT reset the counter when entering Stage 2.
  5. Every candidate's `reasoning` field must explicitly name which stage produced it (e.g. 'Stage 1: located bundle X by directory walk' or 'Stage 2: narrowed to product Y via ODE_search_products bbox filter').

results.title: Kaguya Gamma-Ray Spectrometer Corrected Spectra Bundle

Identification_Area.title: Kaguya Gamma-Ray Spectrometer Corrected Spectra Bundle

Citation_Information.description:
This archive contains Kaguya Gamma-Ray Spectrometer (KGRS) corrected and calibrated time-series 
          spectra and ephemerides, pointing, and geometry (EPG) data for the nominal and extended observation 
          periods of JAXA's SELENE (Kaguya) mission.

          The SELENE mission was the Japanese mission to the Moon with a main orbiter "Kaguya" and
          two daughter relay satellites launched on Sep. 14, 2007. KGRS was mounted on the deck of the
          main orbiter. It employed a high-purity Ge (HPGe) crystal as a main detector, and surrounding
          BGO and plastic scintillators as anti-coincidence detectors to reduce backgrounds. The
          Ge crystal was cooled by a Sterling refrigerator. It made close-proximity observations of the
          Moon from Dec. 14, 2007 to Dec. 11, 2008 at the high circular orbital altitude of 100 km, and
          from Feb. 10 to May 28 in 2009 at the low elliptical orbital altitude of ~30 x 50 km.

          KGRS recorded energy spectra of gamma rays in two gain modes simultaneously; high gain (0.1
          to 3 MeV) and low gain (0.1 to 13 MeV), each of which was analyzed by the 13-bit (8192 ch)
          analog-to-digital converter (ADC). While the former has a higher spectral resolution over the
          energy range of radioactive elements, the latter has better statistics and covers the whole energy
          range of major rock-forming elements, including the Fe neutron capture line at 7.6 MeV. The
          accumulation time of each spectrum was 17 s. Both modes of spectra are included in this archive.

          The observation epochs of KGRS consists of three regular measurement periods (Period 1 to 3),
          background measurements, and the annealing of the Ge crystal. The HPGe detector was annealed
          from 16 to 25, December 2008, which improved energy resolution by a factor of two.
          Period 1 and 2 lasted two and five months, respectively, with limited spatial or spectral
          resolutions. Period 3 lasted ~3.5 months (including dead time) with the best spatial and spectral
          resolutions. In addition, spectra from the background measurement when the satellite was flipped
          and the detector faced deep space can be analyzed to estimate the background gamma-ray intensity
          from the satellite due to the exposure to galactic cosmic rays.

          This archive consists of three spectrum files (each corresponding to one KGRS epoch) and one unified
          EPG file. The spectrum files contain a time series of corrected and calibrated pulse height spectra
          acquired by KGRS's HPGe detector in the lunar orbit. In the spectrum files, each row contains the 
          spacecraft clock (sclk) ticks and UTC time corresponding to the end of the accumulation interval 
          (17 s), with the two sets of the 8192-channel HPGe spectra accumulated. In the EPG file, each row
          starts with sclk recorded at the end of the accumulation interval to match spacecraft positions, 
          pointing, and correction factors with the corrected spectra. Note that the UTC time and ephemeris time
          included in the EPG file correspond to the midpoint of the accumulation interval.

          The Kaguya Gamma-Ray Spectrometer Corrected Spectra bundle was created by Naoyuki Yamashita at Planetary
          Science Institute, AZ, USA, with the permission of JAXA and the KGRS instrument PI Prof. Nobuyuki Hasebe.
          This work was supported by the NASA under Grant No. NNX16AG54G issued through the Planetary Data Archiving,
          Restoration, and Tools Program (PDART), Research Opportunities in Space and Earth Sciences (ROSES) 2015.

parts.args:
{"summary":"To detect/confirm water ice in permanently shadowed regions (PSRs) from orbit you generally need a *combination* of measurements:

1) **Thermal environment**: multi-temporal surface temperature / thermal inertia to identify cold traps where ice is stable (e.g., Diviner calibrated radiances and Diviner polar derived products).
2) **Hydrogen abundance proxy**: epithermal/fast neutron suppression (neutron spectroscopy) to map near-surface hydrogen enrichment consistent with water ice (e.g., LRO LEND; Lunar Prospector NS).
3) **Radar scattering properties**: circular polarization ratio (CPR), Stokes parameters, and bistatic/monostatic backscatter behavior to look for dielectric/volume-scattering signatures consistent with ice (e.g., LRO Mini-RF bistatic/derived products; Clementine bistatic radar).
4) **Topography/illumination context**: high-resolution DEM/altimetry for PSR mapping and illumination modeling (e.g., LRO LOLA gridded altimetry).

Below are the closest matching PDS datasets/collections at the PDS Geosciences node that provide those measurements (calibrated/derived products preferred).","candidates":[{"path":"lro/urn-nasa-pds-lro_diviner_derived1/","dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:lro_diviner_derived1:data_derived_prp","title":"Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter Diviner Polar Resource Product (PRP) Collection","pds_version":"PDS4","mission":"lro","node":"geo","reasoning":"Stage 1: directory-walk located Diviner derived collections.
